title: Emergent Behavior
description: Qualitative capability jumps that appear only past certain model scale or training thresholds—distinct from smooth metric improvements tracked by scaling laws.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Emergent behavior (often called emergent abilities in papers) describes qualitative jumps: a benchmark accuracy curve is flat, then rises quickly once model size or training crosses a threshold. Researchers debate measurement definitions—some jumps shrink when metrics are rescaled—but the term still marks behaviors that were not predictable from small-model trends alone.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Roadmaps and safety reviews care about thresholds where models gain new skills. Scaling laws explain smooth loss trends; emergent behavior highlights tasks where capability seems discontinuous. Generalization and alignment still determine whether those skills help users safely in production.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A multiple-choice reasoning task may sit near chance for 8B models, then climb sharply at 70B while perplexity on web text improved smoothly across sizes—suggesting some skills are not visible from loss alone.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Scaling and capability papers, benchmark suites with sharp scale thresholds, and product announcements about new model tiers. Link back to scaling law for smooth trends and generalization for whether skills transfer beyond the benchmark.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Not every benchmark improvement is emergent—many tasks scale gradually. Emergence is not magic; it can reflect metric nonlinearities or richer pretraining mixtures. It also does not replace alignment: a newly emergent skill can still be misused without policy controls.

openingSummary: Emergent behavior names capabilities absent at smaller scale that appear reliably above a threshold—often debated against smooth metric improvements with scale.
